macro_segments.title: Broadening the Category of Animals: From Shor and Chamor to All Species and the Question of Kilayim

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Wrapping up Perek 5 on Bor and Beginning Perek 6 with Arba Avos Nezikin
Mishna's Extension: Shor and Chamor Includes All Animals, Necessitating Clarification on Kilayim
Reish Lakish's Teaching: Different Species of Birds (Chicken, Peacock, Pheasant) Are Kilayim
Defining Species Boundaries: Tosafos on Rabbi's Implied Categories and the Question of What Makes Species Distinct

macro_segments.title: Determining Species Distinction: From Physical Characteristics to Sexual and Procreative Differences

micro_segments.title: Rav Chaviva: Living Together and Crossbreeding Capability as Indicators of Same or Different Species
Shmuel's Example: Wild Goose vs. Domestic Goose as Distinct Species Despite Similar Names
Ravva bar Bar Chanan's Challenge: Physical Characteristics (Beak, Neck) Are Insufficient to Define Species
Abaye's Resolution: Sexual/Procreative Characteristics (Internal vs. External Testicles) Define Species
Rav Pappa's Addition: Egg-Laying Patterns as Further Evidence of Species Distinction

macro_segments.title: Kilayim in Water: Extending the Prohibition to Sea Animals and the Theological Basis in Bereishis

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmiyah/Reish Lakish: Crossbreeding Two Types of Sea Animals Incurs Lashes
Rav Ada Bar Ahava: Inferring Sea Animal Kilayim from 'LiMinehu' in Bereishis Creation Account
Tosafos Discussion: Whether 'LiMinehu' Alone Establishes Prohibition or Requires Explicit Mitzvah
Theological Intersecting Halacha: Using Non-Halachic Narrative to Define Scope of Mitzvos

macro_segments.title: Practical Application: Crossbreeding Sea and Land Animals (Goat and Fish Drawing Together)

micro_segments.title: Rachava's Question: Goat and Fish/Dolphin Drawing a Wagon—Is This Kilayim?
Abaye and Rava's Dispute: Whether Animals in Separate Spaces (Water/Land) Constitute Kilayim
Ravina's Challenge: Analogy to Seeds in Different Jurisdictions (Eretz Yisrael/Chutz La'Aretz)
Gemara's Response: Different Jurisdictions Have Different Prohibition Zones; Animals' Spaces Are Both Prohibitive

macro_segments.title: Transition to Perek 6: From Bor to Shen, Regel, and Eish; Wrapping Up Shen va-Regel Issues

micro_segments.title: Overview: Perek 6 Covers Shen, Regel, and Eish After Extended Discussion of Shor and Bor
Mishna: Locking Sheep in Pen Appropriately (Karauy) Exempts Owner if Animal Escapes and Damages
Exceptions: Night Breakout and Bandit Removal; Insufficient Guarding (Sun, Deaf/Insane/Minor) Creates Liability
Liability Nuances: Shepherd Assumes Responsibility; Accidental Fall Into Garden Requires Payment for Benefit Received
Assessing Damage: Beit Se'ah Valuation Method for Ungathered Grain vs. Fully Ripened Fruit

macro_segments.title: Defining Karauy: Minimum Guarding Standard of Withstanding Normal Winds (Ruach Metzuya)

micro_segments.title: Tanu Rabanan: Door That Withstands Normal Winds Is Karauy; Inability to Withstand Normal Winds Is Shelo Karauy
Ruach Metzuya as Stock Phrase: Establishing Minimal Shmira as Acceptable Standard

macro_segments.title: Shen va-Regel as Muad Requiring Only Minimal Shmira: Comparing to Keren Muad

micro_segments.title: Rav Mani bar Patish's Question: Which Tanna Teaches That Muad Requires Only Minimal Shmira?
Answer: Rabbi Yehuda—Mishna on Keren Muad Shows Rabbi Yehuda Allows Minimal Shmira for Muad
Gemara Clarification: Even Rabbi Meir Could Hold This for Shen va-Regel, as Torah Reduced Shmira Requirement

macro_segments.title: Four Things with Reduced Shmira: Bor, Eish, Shen, and Regel per Beraita; Keren Stands Apart

micro_segments.title: Beraita: Four Categories Where Torah Reduced Shmira Requirement (Bor, Eish, Shen, Regel)
Bor: Covering Suffices (Kisui Patur) as Minimal Standard from Pasuk 'Lo Yichasenu'
Eish: 'Ad De'avid K'ein Mavir'—Only Liable If Negligence Rises to Level of Intentional Torching
Shen: 'Ad De'avid K'ein U'Bi'eir'—Only Liable If Negligence Rises to Level of Intentional Consumption
Regel: 'Ad De'avid K'ein V'Shilach'—Only Liable If Negligence Rises to Level of Intentionally Sending Animal
Raba's Inference from Mishna Language: Switching to Sheep (Not Ox) Indicates Shen va-Regel Requires Less Shmira

macro_segments.title: Conceptual Framework: Intentional Act of Mazik (Maaseh Mazik) Determines Negligence Threshold

micro_segments.title: Core Logic: Keren Involves Intentional Act of Damage; Other Categories Require High Negligence to Constitute Mazik
By Keren: Animal's Intentional Goring + Any Negligence = Liability; By Others: No Intentional Act, So Need Gross Negligence
Theological/Legal Intersection: Idea That Gross Negligence Becomes 'As If You Did It Yourself' (K'Ein Avid)

macro_segments.title: Four Things Exempt from Human Court but Liable Before Heaven: Poretz Geder, Kofef Kamato, Socher Edim, Yodeya Edus

micro_segments.title: Beraita (Rabbi Yehoshua): Four Acts Are Patur Mi'Dinei Adam but Chayav Bi'Dinei Shamayim
Poretz Geder: Breaking Fence Before Animal; Question of Liability for Fence vs. Animal Loss or Damage
Rashi's Reading: Liable Only for Fence in Case of Strong Wall; Case Involves Weak (Ra'ua) Fence
Tosafot vs. Rambam Alternatives: Dispute on Liability for Lost Animal vs. Only Fence Damage
Rambam: Liable for Animal Damage Only If Intent to Use It for Harm; Parallels Kinyan-Like Possession

macro_segments.title: Kofef Kamato: Placing Grain Before Fire; Liability Based on Foreseeability and Burial (Tomun) Loopholes

micro_segments.title: Case Setup: Placing Sheaf in Front of Fire; Distinction Between Expected (Ruach Metzuya) and Unexpected Fire Spread
Foreseeability Rule: Liable by Dinei Adam If Fire Spread Is Expected (Matzei Ruach Metzuya); Exempt If Unexpected
Rav Ashi: Alternative—Making Grain 'Tomun' (Buried) Under Blanket to Escape Fire Liability
Conceptual Question: Whether Liable for Eish Category or Adam Ha'Mazik When Directly Placing Object Before Inevitable Fire
Tosafot's Analysis: Actively Placing Someone in Harm's Way Is Adam Ha'Mazik (Not Just Eish Liability)
